cases.title: The Febrile Toddler with Cool Extremities
cases.presentation: A 2-year-old, 12 kg boy is brought to the ED by his mother with a history of 4 days of high fever (40.1°C), decreased oral intake, and 2 days of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting and watery diarrhea. The child is lethargic and minimally responsive. On examination, the skin is pale and mottled, extremities are cool, and capillary refill is 5 seconds. Heart rate is 170/min. Blood pressure is 82/50.
cases.question: What type of shock does this child most likely have, and what is the hypotension threshold?
options: A: Hypovolemic shock from dehydration — hypotension threshold is SBP < 74 mmHg
B: Cardiogenic shock — hypotension threshold is SBP < 70 mmHg
C: Distributive (septic) shock — hypotension threshold is SBP < 74 mmHg
D: Obstructive shock — hypotension threshold is SBP < 70 mmHg
cases.explanation: This child has a 4-day febrile illness with vomiting and diarrhea, now presenting with altered mental status, poor perfusion (pale, mottled, cool, CRT 5 sec), and tachycardia. The hypotension threshold is 70 + (2 × 2) = 74 mmHg — his SBP of 82 is above this, so he is in compensated shock. While dehydration contributes, the high fever and systemic illness raise sepsis as the primary distributive cause.
cases.clinicalPearl: In a febrile child with poor perfusion, always consider sepsis even when dehydration is also present. They often coexist and both require treatment.

cases.title: The Infant with a Gallop
cases.presentation: A 6-month-old, 7 kg girl is brought in by ambulance after 3 days of fever, cough, and decreased feeding. Over the past 6 hours, she has become increasingly irritable and then lethargic. On examination, she is pale, mottled, and limp. Heart rate is 200/min. She has a gallop rhythm on cardiac auscultation, an enlarged liver palpated 3 cm below the right costal margin, and bilateral crackles on lung auscultation. Blood pressure is 68/42.
cases.question: What type of shock is this, and what makes it distinct from the most common pediatric shock type?
options: A: Hypovolemic shock — distinct because it has flat neck veins and no gallop
B: Cardiogenic shock — distinct because the pump itself is failing (gallop, hepatomegaly, pulmonary edema) rather than a volume or vasodilation problem
C: Distributive shock — distinct because it has warm, flushed skin
D: Obstructive shock — distinct because it requires pericardiocentesis
cases.explanation: The gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly from venous congestion, pulmonary edema (bilateral crackles, hypoxemia), and the severity of the presentation point to cardiogenic shock — likely from fulminant myocarditis following a viral infection. Unlike distributive or hypovolemic shock, the problem is pump failure: the heart cannot eject blood effectively, causing both forward failure (poor perfusion) and backward failure (congestion).
cases.clinicalPearl: Cardiogenic shock in an infant with a viral prodrome should raise suspicion for myocarditis. Aggressive fluid boluses may worsen pulmonary edema in this setting.

cases.title: The Child After Trauma
cases.presentation: A 9-year-old, 30 kg girl is brought to the ED after a motor vehicle collision. She is conscious but confused. She was the unrestrained back-seat passenger and struck the dashboard. On examination, she is pale and diaphoretic. Her neck veins are flat. Heart rate is 160/min and blood pressure is 84/56. Her abdomen is rigid and tender in all four quadrants. Breath sounds are equal bilaterally.
cases.question: What type of shock does this patient most likely have, and what is the next critical step?
options: A: Cardiogenic shock from cardiac contusion — obtain an echocardiogram
B: Distributive shock from sepsis — start broad-spectrum antibiotics
C: Hypovolemic shock from hemorrhage — obtain blood type and crossmatch, prepare for surgical intervention
D: Obstructive shock from tension pneumothorax — perform needle decompression
cases.explanation: This post-trauma patient with tachycardia, hypotension (hypotension threshold for age 9: 70 + 18 = 88 mmHg; SBP 84 is below this), flat neck veins (low preload), a rigid tender abdomen (intra-abdominal hemorrhage), and altered mental status is in hypovolemic shock from likely intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Equal breath sounds rule out tension pneumothorax. The immediate priorities are IV/IO access, fluid resuscitation, blood product preparation, and emergent surgical consultation.
cases.clinicalPearl: The hypotension threshold for this 9-year-old is SBP < 88 mmHg. Her BP of 84 is hypotensive — she is in hypotensive shock and at imminent risk of cardiac arrest.
